does: This paper develops a model-free reinforcement learning approach for learning multiple behavioral modes in autonomous driving, enabling agents to discover and utilize fallback strategies beyond the primary optimal policy. The core contribution is introducing a pseudo-reward term that encourages exploration toward underexplored regions of the state space, allowing the RL agent to capture diverse modes of behavior rather than converging to a single optimal strategy. The method enables autonomous vehicles to plan alternative courses of action that can be deployed when unexpected environmental changes occur, improving robustness to distribution shifts and unforeseen scenarios that may not appear in the original training data.
connects: This work builds on the classical challenge of learning robust policies in safety-critical domains, extending prior research on multi-modal policy learning and exploration in reinforcement learning. It responds to the limitation that standard RL approaches converge to a single optimal policy, drawing on prior work examining how exploration strategies and intrinsic motivation can help agents discover diverse behavioral modes. The paper connects to research on safe reinforcement learning and contingency planning in autonomous driving, where backup strategies and failure modes must be explicitly considered. It also relates to work on distributional robustness and out-of-distribution generalization, addressing how agents can handle environmental variations beyond their training experience.
enables: This approach enables more robust autonomous vehicle control systems capable of adapting to unexpected scenarios through pre-learned fallback strategies. The method opens research directions in hierarchical decision-making for AVs, where the agent first executes the primary policy but seamlessly transitions to learned alternatives when anomalies are detected. It supports future work in safety verification and validation of autonomous systems by providing a systematic way to generate diverse behavioral modes for testing. The technique could extend to other safety-critical domains requiring contingency planning, such as robotics and industrial control, and enables investigation of how multi-modal policies improve resilience to catastrophic failures and enable graceful degradation in autonomous systems.
